Client 2: Works.
Advisor: James, Linda—when you think of your household gross income for the year, what number should I use right now?
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-sixteen a year. The exact we’ve been using is 116150.
Advisor: Got it—116150 gross household income. And just so I’m not mixing sources, James, you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client 1: Yeah—unemployed right now.

Client 2: Bonus, annual. The annual amount we’re using is 70100.
Advisor: Thanks—let me note that down. Now switching to spending: what are your total monthly expenses, all-in—housing, utilities, groceries, everything?
Client 2: Total monthly expenses are 4250.
Advisor: Okay—4250 per month. And on top of that, do you have any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client 2: Works.
Advisor: James, Linda—when you think of your household gross income for the year, what number should I use right now?
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-sixteen a year. The exact we’ve been using is 116150.
Advisor: Got it—116150 gross household income. And just so I’m not mixing sources, James, you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client 1: Yeah—unemployed right now.

Client 2: No, loans outstanding are zero.
Advisor: Great. Let’s move to assets—this is the part where we’ll list the accounts and rough balances. What do you consider your investable assets total—so brokerage, retirement, cash that’s earmarked for investing?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 361750.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. Now I’ll break that down—tell me if I miss anything. Do you have a joint taxable investment account on an advisor platform?
Client 2: Yes—joint taxable on the advisor platform, value is 201550.

Client 1: Yeah, private markets there too. Value is 32350.
Advisor: And then your home—your primary residence—what value are you using for that property?
Client 2: Primary residence value is 212950, held at the bank.
Advisor: Thanks. Last two categories and we’ll pause: liabilities we already covered as none, and protection—do you currently have any life insurance or disability policies we should list today?
Client 1: Not right now—no policies to list.

Client 2: Works.
Advisor: James, Linda—when you think of your household gross income for the year, what number should I use right now?
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-sixteen a year. The exact we’ve been using is 116150.
Advisor: Got it—116150 gross household income. And just so I’m not mixing sources, James, you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client 1: Yeah—unemployed right now.

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. Honestly, I want a clear plan that accounts for the fixed stuff—especially from my prior marriage—without making us feel boxed in.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, accounts—so we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start high level with income, then we’ll go spending, then assets, and I’ll ask about any debts and insurance at the end. Sound okay?

Client 2: US.
Advisor: Perfect. Now relationship status—how should I describe it in the plan? Married, remarried, partnered—what’s the right wording for you two?
Client 1: We’re secondly wedded.
Client 2: Yeah—secondly wedded. Second marriage for both of us.
Advisor: Thanks. And then how you found us and how we’ll work together—are you engaging me through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship?

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. Honestly, I want a clear plan that accounts for the fixed stuff—especially from my prior marriage—without making us feel boxed in.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, accounts—so we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start high level with income, then we’ll go spending, then assets, and I’ll ask about any debts and insurance at the end. Sound okay?

Client 1: Moderate. I mean, I want growth, but with the obligations I don’t want to get too cute.
Advisor: Makes sense. And taxes—what bracket are we using as the working assumption for you two right now?
Client 2: 37%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. While we’re on the “zoomed out” stuff, I know we said we’d detail income next—can I just confirm the two income items we’re capturing so I don’t miss one?
Client 1: Sure.

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. Honestly, I want a clear plan that accounts for the fixed stuff—especially from my prior marriage—without making us feel boxed in.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, accounts—so we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start high level with income, then we’ll go spending, then assets, and I’ll ask about any debts and insurance at the end. Sound okay?
Client 1: Yep.

Advisor: James, on your side, do you have a workplace retirement account—401(k) or IRA—held at a retirement platform?
Client 1: Yes. It’s at a retirement platform, and the value is 73400.
Advisor: Any other investments in your name—like private markets or alternatives—also at that retirement platform?
Client 1: Yeah, private markets there too. Value is 32350.
Advisor: And then your home—your primary residence—what value are you using for that property?

Advisor: Great. And for planning assumptions, what state are you resident in right now?
Client 1: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Got it. Now, risk—when markets swing, how would you describe your overall comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits, James and Linda?
Client 1: Moderate. I mean, I want growth, but with the obligations I don’t want to get too cute.
Advisor: Makes sense. And taxes—what bracket are we using as the working assumption for you two right now?

Advisor: Okay. That gives me the household frame. Next we’ll drill into the income amounts and timing, but this helps a lot.
Advisor: Alright—let’s drill into the income amounts and who they belong to. James, starting with you: what are we using as your gross annual income right now?
Client 1: For me, gross annual income is 89700.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing terms—employment-wise at the moment, are you working, between roles, consulting… what’s the status?
Client 1: I’m unemployed right now.

Advisor: Any other investments in your name—like private markets or alternatives—also at that retirement platform?
Client 1: Yeah, private markets there too. Value is 32350.
Advisor: And then your home—your primary residence—what value are you using for that property?
Client 2: Primary residence value is 212950, held at the bank.
Advisor: Thanks. Last two categories and we’ll pause: liabilities we already covered as none, and protection—do you currently have any life insurance or disability policies we should list today?

Advisor: James, Linda—when you think of your household gross income for the year, what number should I use right now?
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-sixteen a year. The exact we’ve been using is 116150.
Advisor: Got it—116150 gross household income. And just so I’m not mixing sources, James, you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client 1: Yeah—unemployed right now.
Advisor: And Linda, you’re currently employed?

Advisor: Hi James, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. Honestly, I want a clear plan that accounts for the fixed stuff—especially from my prior marriage—without making us feel boxed in.

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And then work-wise, what bucket would you put your occupation in? Like healthcare, education, tech—whatever fits best.
Client 2: Healthcare.
Advisor: Perfect. And James, for you—separate question—what bucket fits your occupation background best? Same idea: healthcare, finance, business owner, that kind of thing.
Client 1: Business owner.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to connect the dots with what we already captured: you two still have those two income items—James’s rental income monthly and Linda’s annual bonus—plus the five assets we listed, zero liabilities, and no protection policies. Still all consistent?

Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner is right.
